I believe fitness industry peeps are the best entrepreneurs and mog all the larpers 

{{PERSON}}, {{PERSON}}, {{PERSON}}, {{PERSON}}, {{PERSON}}, {{PERSON}}, the list goes on endlessly 

If you can’t stay in shape and claim to make bank, I’m just not buying it in most cases 

Not because a six pack magically make you rich

But because getting in shape requires a lot of the same traits that build a business

Discipline

Consistency

Delayed gratification

The ability to do boring things for years without immediate reward

The fitness industry is one of the few industries where you can’t completely fake it

You can rent a {{PERSON}}

You can flex revenue screenshots (many such cases)

But it’s very difficult to fake a decade of training

When you enter the fitness scene, you almost automatically become an entrepreneur

You learn marketing because nobody sees your content otherwise

You learn sales because nobody buys your coaching otherwise

You learn branding because there are a thousand people doing the same thing

You learn leadership because clients need guidance

The average fitness coach is creating content, running sales, {{LOCATION}}, managing clients, building systems and trying to stay in shape at the same time

Most people struggle with one of those

Fitness entrepreneurs are juggling all of them

Most couldn’t survive six months doing it

And if someone genuinely has an incredible physique, a great family life, a successful business and has managed to maintain all of it for years…

You’re a beast (me)

I enjoy various styles of exercise 

But nothing beats training for hypertrophy + getting a pump {{URL}}

Almost 1 year of being a father  

Here’s a few things I’ve learned 

Your relationship with your wife is beyond important, put her first and make her life as easy as possible

Because your child grows up in the world you create together, the better your relationship is, the better everything else tends to be 

Spending time with your daughter or son is the most productive thing you can do 

You will have less time for work and life in general but you will learn to be better and more efficient with the time you do have 

When you become a father, you have this constant proud feeling, you are building your family, you feel you’re on the right path 

You care less about status games and impressing others, you just want your wife and kids to think you’re cool so you prioritise them and stop chasing others, ironically by doing this other people will think you’re cooler 

From a fitness perspective, a lot of dads talk about fatherhood as if it’s the reason they got out of shape.

Sounds harsh, but in reality, most weren’t in great shape beforehand and they use fatherhood as an excuse, not good to blame family life, if anything it should motivate you 

Your kids are watching, your wife is watching

And one day your son or daughter will decide what normal looks like by looking at you, so set the standard 

Being a {{PERSON}}, healthy dad who decides his own schedule is the ultimate flex in life

In my (biased) opinion

This is the perfect marbella weekend with the right mix of business, productivity, fitness and just having a good time

USER_500baec7f0: It's amazing how many people do not know how to be kind to themselves, and yet think that they can be kind to others. How can you know what kindness is if you have not tried it on for yourself? {{URL}}

It’s interesting how often we think about changing things and then don’t because…well I don’t know how or I’ll probably get it wrong or fail or it’s too hard. As my friend Lisa would say - ‘just put on your big girl/boy pants and get on with it!’ {{URL}}

The kindness I find most intriguing is self kindness. Until I started working as a {{PERSON}} had thought it was just me that had mean, cruel, abusive thinking. I now know it’s not. And self kindness is a way out. Practise being kind to you, as you would someone you truly love! {{URL}}
Did you know that studies have been down with children under two years old, and more than 85% of the time in situations, they will naturally be kind. It seems to be that we are made that way. {{URL}}
We started the month off yesterday looking at triggers and how to deal with them when they happen. {{LOCATION}} they do! Check the website for how to become a member for £1! {{URL}} {{URL}}
What if you tried that one for size this year? Not just as a new year resolution fad, but really said ‘yeah, I’m into altering my attitude to change my life.’ {{URL}}

Each year I take the time to complete the ‘end of year review’. I created it years ago for clients, and those on my mailing list (if you’d like a copy DM the word REVIEW). Pause before you move into a new year and contemplate the past 12 months, what can you learn from that? {{URL}}

Regrets do not change the past. They do impact the present though. When you look to your past for what you can learn, it’s easier to move forward with strength. So take a moment to gather those lessons. x {{URL}}

It may be ‘just another day’, but you can choose to play! What type of play brings lightness? Ideas below please, not everyone finds this easy. Play for me is dancing, playing with my dog, making things, painting and doing what I can when I can to be creative. What is it for you? {{URL}}
Take a deep breath. As you let it out feel what that’s like in your body. Repeat, and again. Now you are here, what do you notice? {{URL}}
It really is! {{URL}}

{{USER_MENTION}} is 10! As we heard from the founder last night, it takes a team for a dream to come to be. And ten years on it's still going strong. I am honoured to volunteer and be able to share the night with such great women (and yes wear the same dress as a guest too!) 😂😂 {{URL}}

One of my friends is an ex Olympian, she coaches up coming athletes…for her fun IS being on the track in all weathers. For me…not so much 😂 What is fun for you may not be fun for someone else, that’s what is so wonderful about mankind, we are so varied. {{URL}}
What does doing it right mean for you? For me, it’s about honesty and being present with life…which actually takes more practise than it sounds! {{URL}}
Investing in you, imho, is one of the most wonderful things you can do. Be it learning a new craft, language, job, or going deeper spiritually. So rewarding. {{URL}}

{{LOCATION}}. {{PERSON}} was a British teenager kidnapped into slavery in Ireland.

At about 16 years old, Irish raiders captured him and forced him to herd sheep alone in the wilderness for six years. Cold, hungry, and isolated, he turned to God—praying constantly. He later wrote that his faith was forged in that suffering.

After a daring escape and return home, {{PERSON}} had a dream: the Irish people were calling him back. He could have stayed safe and remained bitter towards them for the wrong they had done to him. But instead… he went back to share the gospel.

Patrick spent the rest of his life preaching Christ across Ireland, {{LOCATION}}, {{LOCATION}}, and confronting pagan practices. Many credit his ministry with helping transform Ireland from widespread paganism to Christianity.

And the famous “snakes” he supposedly drove out? Ireland never had literal snakes—the story likely symbolizes the driving out of pagan worship and druid practices.

So St. Patrick’s Day isn’t really about luck, leprechauns, or green beer. It’s about forgiveness, courage, and a former slave who returned to the land of his captivity to bring the message of Jesus.

A Letter to the Next Generation, and to the Mothers and Fathers of Faith:

In the {{NUMBER}}s, we began gathering young people on college campuses across {{LOCATION}}. We called these small weekend events Rock The Nations—simple gatherings with one purpose: to activate a generation and their leaders to take back America and their schools for Jesus.

As momentum grew, we knew God was calling us deeper. We launched PrayerStorm, where thousands of young people gathered to fast, pray, and cry out for America and the Church. None of us could have imagined that those seeds would begin to birth what we are now seeing across the United States.

PrayerStorm culminated in {{NUMBER}} with The Call on the National Mall in Washington, D.C. That day, nearly 800,000 students, parents, and leaders came together to fast, pray, and call down heaven for our nation. Every one of these gatherings was grounded in Malachi 4:5-6—the final passage of the Old Testament:

"See, I will send the prophet Elijah to you before that great and dreadful day of the Lord comes. He will turn the hearts of the fathers to their children, and the hearts of the children to their fathers."

We had seen something like this before. In the {{NUMBER}}s, the Jesus Movement swept across America, with thousands coming to Christ in miraculous ways. But the “fathers” of the institutional church at the time didn’t like the disruption, the messiness, or the attention—it was shut down too soon. A few, like {{PERSON}} and {{PERSON}}, recognized God’s hand, but the conservative church of the day could not see past its discomfort.

Now, in this moment, with the assassination of Charlie Kirk, a generation has been jolted awake once again. The Spirit of Elijah is moving. {{PERSON}} people are rising—bold, {{LOCATION}}, courageous, and unwilling to back down. They will take back their schools, breathe life into dead churches, and carry Jesus into stadiums, cities, and nations.

But this time, it cannot be shut down. Why? Because they understand they need spiritual mothers and fathers—not to control them, not to silence them, but to walk beside them, cheer them on, and turn our hearts toward them as they turn theirs toward us. Together, we will see a movement that is not only powerful but generational.
The only revival in history that lasted beyond one generation was the Moravian Movement, which burned for 100 years. I believe we are on the brink of something even greater.

Are you ready? The 42nd generation is about to cross over. God is about to cover the whole earth with His glory.

Hold on—this is just the beginning. {{USER_MENTION}}

This week, I heard it all: that President {{PERSON}} because he was a threat and planning to start his own political party. That Charlie was having an affair with {{PERSON}}. That Ericka would not have forgiven the shooter if he were any other color than white.

And then, when I simply asked people to show me any videos of Charlie being racist or homophobic, I was told I was ignorant and uneducated. The same people admitted, “I would never watch his videos—he was too disgusting.”

So, where did they form their opinions? TikTok.

Friends, this is madness. Put your phones down. Read a book. Do your own research. Don’t let someone else’s outrage or narrative become your truth.

I want to start this post by saying I am truly sorry for the pain so many are experiencing right now. We know how hard life can be. For Lisa and me, that pain and grief run deep. Our Michael would have been 31 this year — he was born to be a {{PERSON}} until drugs, mental illness, and the true enemy stole that from him. 

And many of you know Lisa’s story — widowed at 25 with two little girls — so she understands profoundly the pain Erika is carrying for herself and her children.

Like Erika, {{PERSON}} is a woman of strength, {{LOCATION}}, forgiveness, beauty, and tenacity. Our whole adult lives have been about raising up men like Charlie and women like Erika — people who would fill the earth with God’s glory and raise the standard in their generation and the Church.

With a couple of my posts, I’ve been accused by alumni, by some in my own family, and by many on social media of being ignorant, racist, homophobic, and worse. At various stages of my life, some of those labels may have been true. But they are not true now. At 59, with nothing left to prove in ministry, finances, or work, I don’t have much left to defend. But I do have something left to fight for.

And when I say fight, I don’t mean politics, religion, or people. I mean fighting the demonic voices that have crippled generation after generation — the lies that silence fathers and keep men from walking in love, integrity, and truth.

I know many in the younger generation are angry — at the Church, at their parents, {{EMAIL}}. Much of that anger comes from the shame we’ve put on you. I hear the voice of shame you are wrestling with: “I should have been a {{PERSON}}/Erika, but I wasn’t — so now I’ll demonize anyone who is for them.” 

Please hear me: We are not worshiping a {{PERSON}}. We are not racist. We are not homophobic. If you’ve experienced our lives at any level, you know that.

We are simply two people who walk with a limp, and we will continue to give our lives to break the lies that are being whispered in so many ears. Because if you are yelling and hating others, that’s not the Father’s voice. That’s another voice.

A friend reminded me today that the Spirit says, “If you are not loving, encouraging, and finding sympathy for all, you are not hearing the voice of the Father. Any other voice will deceive and distract you.” And God is going to use those in this time that only hear His voice, not the voice of hatred. 

I know I’ll receive hate for posting this. That’s okay. Just know: I love you. And I will always fight for you, never against you.
